speaker: interviewer
text: The date is December 4, 1996. The survivor is Frieda Stieglitz, maiden name Einsiedler. The interviewer is Adele Abolafia. We are in Queens in the United States of America. And the language is English. My name is Adele Abolafia, A-D-E-L-E A-B-O-L-A-F-I-A. Today is December 4, 1996. And I am interviewing Frieda Stieglitz, born Einsiedler. We are in Queens, New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good morning, Mrs. Stieglitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please spell your first and last name and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My name is Frieda Stieglitz. And you spell it S-T-I-E-G-L-I-T-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my, my, my, my maiden name is Einsiedler. And you spell it E-I-N-S-I-E-D-L-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I did not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you give your date of birth, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born May the 30th, 1933.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what town were you born in-- and country?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Grodzisko Dolne, [SIGHS] in the country of Poland. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: --you spell it G-R-O-D-Z-I-S-K-O. And the, the second, Dolne, is D-O-L-N-E. This means 'the lower part'-- Dolne.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe the house that you lived in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We lived in the house-- before the war, it was very sort of primitive looking. It wasn't made from bricks. It was-- I believe most of the houses were from, from-- most of the houses were made from, from, like, wood. There were still some log cabins, real log cabins, in the village. And there still are today. And some houses in that village had-- I believe the non-Jewish people lived. They had straw roofs yet. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your house like? How many rooms?

speaker: interviewee
text: It, it, it-- you see, when my father left, my mother sort of-- I don't know if we lived there before my father left. But when my father left, we lived in a-- not many rooms, but a very huge, large room, which accommodated the kitchen. And there was, like, a wood burning stove in there, very old-fashioned, which some people like today. I wish I had it. And you had to bring in the wood inside the house. It was near the side of the house. I remember there was always the-- you had to buy this wood, chopped up for-- ready log to put into the stove. And, and, and in this room, it was almost like a bedroom, it-- that was. Two beds, single beds, and there was nice furniture, blond, I remember. It was blond furniture, very modern looking for those days, very modern. And, and there was a nightstand and two beds. And even my mother had a dressing-- which I don't even have today such a pretty one. She had a beautiful, kidney-shaped dressing room, dressing table. And I remember there was standing beautiful silk flowers on it, green with-- I get the chills when I describe them to you because they were so pretty. My mother had wonderful taste in things. She had the most modern taste, up-to-date. She was living in a village, but she had the taste of a big-city lady. That was about her that I, I confirmed by somebody else that told me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents' names-- my father was Ephraim. My, my, my, my grandmother called him [NON-ENGLISH] in Jewish. But his real name was Ephraim. And my mother's name was Gittel. But her English-- I mean her Polish name, that the way-- officially she was called Gisella, which was a very popular name. And-- but-- and, and her parents called her Gittel. And everybody in the family called her Gittel, who-- when you were talking, you know, among Jewish, Jewish-- anybody she knew Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, OK-- you see, my father wasn't as [LAUGHS] straight as my mother because my grandparents-- his parents-- had a little mill. They had little mill, which was operated by hand. It wasn't by water or electricity. There was no electricity in those-- but they-- it had a handle. And they worked hard. And they turned it by handle. It was a mill that milled kasha. Until today, if you come that-- to the village and you ask some neighbors, they will call my grandmother not by her name, but they remember her by-- call her [NON-ENGLISH], which means the one who-- who ground the, the, the, the-- I guess the kasha or whatever. And you know, she was a very hardworking woman, my grandmother, with her husband. They worked side by side. And on the side, in the same house, they had their own house, a larger house than we lived in. They had their own house. It sat on a big piece of ground. And-- and it had also a little store in the same house. So there was, like, a little store that had all sorts of notions and even maybe some sugar and flour that immediately, if you wanted to buy something, you know, that-- because they weren't too many-- very few stores in that area. This was in the-- away from the main what you call the shtetl, where the Jewish people lived, where there were some stores. This was-- in this area, they were not stores. So my grandmother had some things like needles and threads and all kinds of-- and candy. That was the one that I liked the best. I used to go into the-- and there was a whole shelf of jars with candy. And my grandmother, that was-- those are my most wonderful memories between me and my grandmother, when she would take me, open up the store.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she would say, which candy do you want? And I-- she would go to one of the jars and it's like saying, eeny, meeny, miny, moe, which one? It was hard to decide because I was a big nosher. And I had to tell her which candy I wanted. And that was my memory of-- that's what they did. They had a little store, and they had a mill-- a little mill. And did your father work there, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father must have helped them when he was young. Because he was not young when he got married. He was not very young. See, he remained an only child. He wasn't born an only child because there were three children. And what happened, so sad-- that my father's brother died when he was very small, like, maybe bar mitzvah age. Maybe, I'm not even sure. Because it was way long before I was born. And my-- and my father's sister was a beautiful girl. And she died just at the age when she was supposed to get almost married. She was, like, a-- [? college ?] age. And she was-- which is like a, a girl who's just right to get married. It was that age. And she died. And I heard it from non-Jewish people, later in the war, how-- what an accomplished young woman she was. She knew everything, how to sew. My grandparents had a sewing machine in the house. Not everybody had a sewing-- a Singer Sewing machine. And she made her own clothes. And she knew how to-- do everything. She was very accomplished, and she was very pretty. Because the goyim, they had no reason why to tell me she was pretty. And they told me she was dark, it was a brunette, and she was pretty, and she knew many things. And she apparently was known, because people far away from my grandparents knew her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was known. She must have been, you know, a young-- nice young adult. And unfortunately, she died from a sickness. Do you know her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, her name was also, I believe, Gittel. I, I think-- I think her name was also Gittel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your grandmother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother's name was Chaya. And, and she was-- she came from a very, very, very distinguished, good family. Her parents were-- her father was a [NON-ENGLISH] from Lizhensk, which is a very known-- in Polish, it's Lezajsk. But in Jewish, people called it Lizhensk. And Rav Elimelech was buried in that town. Until today, people come and visit the grave. And my grandmother was born there. And her parents owned land in the nearby village-- smaller than a village, it's it's called [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH]. My father used to pronounce it [NON-ENGLISH]. But it wasn't. When I went back, I realized it started with a R, and it was [NON-ENGLISH]. And-- and it's a-- it's smaller than a village. And, and that's where they owned land, my grandmother's parents. And their name was Katz. And they came from a very good family. Because not every family owned land. And I still have the, the amount of land they owned. I have a deed. Because my father sent for it from America to, to, to claim for it. And he couldn't get it. But they were landowners. And they were-- they were from very good stock, from the [NON-ENGLISH]. In other words, my grandmother was a [NON-ENGLISH] from a very good family. And she was a very, very strong lady. I don't-- have never met anybody that I-- when I came to-- like her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when I came to America, I was-- I was very young. And I said, who am I going to model myself after? Because I wanted-- I felt since I survived, maybe God had meant special something-- had special something to choose-- I should become something. I should do something with my life. And I-- somehow, I didn't. I didn't become any professional. And I was thinking, who can I model myself? And I went to the library, and I said, who can I choose to model myself after, that I should become somebody really, you know, somebody good and, and do important things? But you know what? Now that I look back, I don't think I need anybody except my grandmother. She was the greatest model for me because she had strength like nobody that I ever met. She had the strength to do everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was good and kind. She, she was Orthodox. She was-- She didn't cheat. She worked hard all her life. She baked her own bread and cooked her own meals. And she, she worked side by side, next to my grandfather. And she, she didn't have time even to talk [NON-ENGLISH], which is bad words. She had no time for that. On Shabbos, she would read the [NON-ENGLISH]. And she was the most wonderful woman that I could ever aspire to be. And she was-- till the end, she proved that she was like not the ordinary woman. Because she didn't do-- she didn't go willingly to death. She was-- she didn't give herself up like, like a sheep. She went into hiding, and she hid us. And because of that, I'm sitting here and talking to you. Because it was because of her that I survived, in the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we go to that, let's-- before we get to the point--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is about my grandma. I have a lot to say about this grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: When we go back to the time when you're still small, and from the first memories that you have, do you remember any brothers or sisters? What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no brothers. But my parents were very young when my father went, so to speak, to America, left Poland. Because he thought he's going to improve our life and bring us over to America. So he left, so they-- their marriage--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did he leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1937. And he-- so their marriage was interrupted. And therefore, at that point, they had three children, three girls. So I have two sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest was Cirelle, they called her. And it's not [NON-ENGLISH] but Cirelle, I know that for sure. And it's not from the name [NON-ENGLISH]. But it's a certain, unusual name, Cirelle. And my, my-- and she was very pretty. And she was blond. And the little sister was, was-- her name was Raizel. And she was-- she was my youngest sister. And she was-- she was the baby. And, and that's all we had. We were three sisters when their marriage was interrupted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when your father left for the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I do not. I was about four, approximately four, four and a half. And I don't remember that at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your first memories of your--

speaker: interviewee
text: My first memories was that my mother always talked about my father. And she wrote letters to him. And she would take my hand in hers on Friday, and she would guide my hand in hers-- because I didn't know-- I was-- I was a little girl. I didn't go to school yet. And she would guide my hand. And she would say, now-- she would write a letter. And then she would guide my hand to say the last few words-- regards-- that I'm, I'm saying goodbye to my father. And this is the way I remember, yes. And I remember that my father was very learned. And he left the Gemara on the little table, not the table where we ate, but on, like a-- like a card table. And the Gemara was never removed from this table, as if his presence was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I knew-- and I always used to look. I sometimes opened up the Gemara, and I saw my father's handwriting even. And he had a beautiful handwriting. And he really-- he knew Hebrew like a sofer. He could write.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I used to see this Gemara. And his presence was there. And I remember my father never even removed his watch. He left it on the night table, as if he-- as if he was going get up in the morning, and he is going to pick up the watch, a golden watch. And he left it. It wasn't even hidden. It was just-- it was just laying on this night table. So I knew my father's-- I knew that he's there. We-- just that we will rejoin him in-- in the near future. That's what I remember of my-- my mother always made us aware of my father-- talked about him, what he was like. And you know and-- and of course, my grandmother, who-- he was the only son, she would always talk about-- I would be always aware that this was her only learned son. And I was her granddaughter. So I was always being made aware that my father wasn't just somewhere-- he was somewhere, but not-- his presence was always near, near. That's what I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your house a religious home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our house was very religious. My mother came from a very religious home. And her father was a Hasid of the Bluzhiver Rebbe. And the-- many dynasties-- in other words, many generations of, of the Bluzhiver dynasty my grandfather followed. He was one of his biggest supporters, one of his biggest followers, the closest. He was so close that every holiday we'd go-- and I believe in, in some holidays, I don't now if all-- and I know he was a-- the Bluzhiver Rebbe, before he died, I met him. And he remembered what my grandfather said to him. He was very learned. The Dvar Torah-- he said to him. When-- before he died, he said he remembered the Dvar Torah-- my grandfather said to the Bluzhiver Rav. And he came to see him when he became the rav. And my zayde, my grandfather, my mother's father was very Orthodox. He had a beard-- and he, he wore a beard. And he was very Hasidic. And he was a nice man. And people liked him. And somebody told me he was even considered somewhat, in her exact word in Yiddish, somewhat not poor. In other words, he, he had-- he was considered somewhat-- for that village, he was considered not poor but somewhat, you know, affluent, in a way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Jossel Susswein. And it's a very unusual name. Because anybody who spelled it this-- that way-- S-U-S-S, with a little dot on the top, on the U-- this was the from the same family originated. And the way I found out why this is spelled like this-- because the first grandfather Susswein that we know of made wine. And he, he came from near Lemberg. And he settled in, in the town of Lesko, which is very far south in Poland, near Czechoslovakia. And he settled there. And he-- they-- and he-- that's why he was named Susswein, Susswein. And that's the reason why the name was-- you know, when names were given, way back in the centuries. That's where the name originates. Susswein-- and I love that name. I think it's pretty, sounds pretty. And I love that grandfather. And my mother had so much respect for her parents. She was the-- very respectful and loved her parents very much. And she was the oldest. And, and I remember such fond memories. I was so rich when I had these grandparents. I felt so rich when I had them. It was the most happiest memories, just go to visit them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was a typical Shabbos like in your house that you remember from--

speaker: interviewee
text: You see--

speaker: interviewer
text: --when you were a little girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, the typical Shabbos was like this. It was somewhat spoiled because my father's presence wasn't there. But my mother kept up everything that was in her power to keep up. She baked the challahs on Friday herself. She baked the rugelach, which I can still smell and see in front of my eyes. I-- and right-- and she, she cleaned up the house. It was Shabbistic. It was beautiful. And-- and then she would sit down and write a letter to my father and sometimes to her aunt. And this is-- Shabbos was quiet and peaceful. And the village was-- assumed a beautiful-- Jewish people lived, you know, a few hundred. And it was quiet. And, and the goyim didn't-- knew that the Jews are Jews. And they go to shul with stramel. You see men with stramel. And I have a picture here, I have a book which shows how it looked, Shabbos. It-- what I don't remember, it describes in this book. Because this-- Mr. Beller, the painter painted what Shabbos looks like in Grodzisko. So he-- he confirmed what I remember. Shabbos was quiet. There was no noise. There was-- there were no cars. And the few-- you hardly saw a horse or a wagon. And people walked in the middle of the street to go to shul in the morning. In the afternoon, they carried the cholent, and it smelled in the streets. You walked-- you walked to the baker. And I used to pick up the cholent with my sister. And it smelled like heaven. We had to carry it from, from the oven, from the communal oven. Because there was an oven that there was no other-- that everybody could put the cholent in there. And that was so peaceful, after you came from shul. And it was beautiful and peaceful and nice. And the Jewish people, were-- they knew-- the goyim knew, the non-Jews knew that Jews observe Sabbath. They knew. There was even a Shabbos goy. If you needed somebody to light-- if something went wrong, there were certain people knew that they have to do something for the, you know, that Jewish people didn't do. And that's the way it was. And, and, and, and there was, was-- there was rest and reading. And the women, those that read, that sat around and read-- and sat around those that liked to read, non-- besides read other things, like Shalom Aleichem, on some of the afternoon. And it was-- it was so beautiful that I would give anything to, to get just one day back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you and your sisters do on, on Shabbos? What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we-- we-- I don't remember. Yeah, I would sometimes walk with my grandparents. I would walk by myself a half an hour, 20 minutes, I don't remember exactly how long. But I knew my way. And I didn't have to be afraid that a car is going to run me over. There were no cars then, hardly. You never saw a car. The only-- and-- and even the horse and wagon went at a pace that you weren't afraid of. If a man was sitting on a wagon, he would-- you know, I would walk on the side, and nothing happened. I could walk, and nobody would ever kidnap me. I was never afraid of kidnapping. It was-- it-- you walked, and I used to-- it was beautiful. I would walk and watch at the grass and the surroundings. There were fields and flowers. And I would walk to my grandparents. And I knew when I came there, I-- my welcome-- I would be welcomed very much. They loved me so much, more than-- except for my mother. And-- I-- my life was ideal. Nothing was missing in my life before the war, absolutely nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember in Grodzisko, do you know how many people were the total population there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe 300, maybe, perhaps. But I can-- I was a child. And I'm only asking now people that survived. They say around 300. I don't know exactly because I was a little girl. And I was-- I didn't even remember the names. Some names I remember, a few, but not too many. Because I was a child then. Before the war I was a little girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, you know, little girls just play. And what do they do? They don't think of how many people live there or something like this. This you'll have to leave to the historians or whoever. I'll leave this to other people who were older than me. As a little girl, do you remember ever playing with non-Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, I, I came across but not really playing. Because, you know, the Jewish people, I think, kept to themselves, and the non-Jewish play-- you know, the Jewish children were more--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever taught that you shouldn't play with--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really--

speaker: interviewer
text: --with non-Jewish, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I wasn't, No, I wasn't-- no, and I wasn't because they lived on both sides. Next to us  lived on both sides non-Jewish people. The Jewish-- non-Jewish people lived next to each other in, in that village. There was the next-door neighbor across the street, that it-- it wasn't like that. It's just that we knew our place. We knew our place, that we were Jewish. In other words, you see, we knew that we were Jewish. So that-- let's say Shabbos. You didn't want-- you had to go to shul, and they didn't. We had to eat kosher, and they didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we knew our place. Even as a child, I knew, you know. And, and the ones that I met, like a boy, I remember he always-- I was afraid of him. They, they actually teased Jewish children. They were, like, annoying Jewish children. The non-Jewish boys-- Like what would they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They would annoy us. They would either throw-- so, like, snow, where I would be afraid of this boy when I-- when I did see. I was intimidated by them, you know. They intimidated the Jewish girls when they saw us. In other words, if I had to pass by this Jew-- I remember, this non-Jew-- he-- I was afraid of him. They always, you know, started up with the Jewish children. And I, I was always avoiding. And they didn't do anything serious.

speaker: interviewer
text: But nevertheless, it was-- it was annoying to-- because they always started up whether it was Jewish girls or Jewish boys, you know. So Jewish boys kept with other-- that-- for that reason. They had to-- they had to keep with other Jewish boys because they, they shouldn't be annoyed by the non-Jewish. You understand what I'm saying? So that's what it was like. Even though they were neighbors, non-Jew-- but everybody they knew their place. They knew they were non-Jew. We knew we were Jewish. And that's the way it was. What do you remember as a child from your nice life that you had? When do you remember things starting to change, from a child's point of view?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first time I heard bad things in my life was when my mother mentioned that we were going to have to put, like, protection on the glass, like tape, so in case the bombs fall or the gas or something, it shouldn't break and fall. And this was the very first time I heard. Hitler was in power, but I didn't know it, I didn't read a paper yet then. I had no way-- there was no radios. And there were no loudspeakers or anything in our village.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the first thing I heard was when my mother said-- she actually went through the process of putting tape on the window. And this was a warning that something is-- bad is going to happen, that war-- war, the, the, the, the sound of war, in other words, it will be coming. And did you ask her about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did-- what did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: --I didn't have to ask her. I felt frightened. I felt frightened. I understood already what she meant. I understood. When she said bombs or gas, I understood what she said. I didn't ask her. That was the very first what I heard about war or bad times or Hitler. In other words, Hitler was mentioned, but what he was trying to do. In other words, what he was bringing to us, to the world-- or the part of the world where I lived. And it was very frightening. It was very unsettling. It was very-- what-- it was very different from what I was until then used to, normalcy, you know, the normal way of life.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your sister feel? Did you ever talk about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it's funny. You know, the thing is that I don't remember my sister-- I think she was at that time by my other grandparents. I was told later. I didn't remember that. That, I blocked out. My sister must have been-- at that time, my older sister was sent to my other grandparents in Dubiecko. When my, my father left, you know, that my mother should keep on with the business-- be able to carry on and make a living for herself. She-- my oldest sister went to live, I believe, with my-- with my other grandparents. And that's why I can't remember that much. Because it wasn't until the war started that, that she joined us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- at a certain, maybe year, that I didn't see her, or something. A certain period of time after my father left, she, she lived with my grandparents. And they-- and she was, like, one of that family.

speaker: interviewee
text: In other words, they-- she-- they adored her. And, and I was very jealous. I was envious that she, she was there because she had her own garden and everything. And I was envious. Till today, I can't forget that she had such a privilege of staying there with a garden. And they adored her, all the aunts and the uncles. And, and, and it was such a pretty little, charming little city. And I, I, I envied her, that she was sent there. But I had my other grandparents. They, they, they knew me better because, see, I had the privilege of knowing this grandparents because I used to-- I could go to them, walk by myself. So I, I, I, you know, I, I had the other-- I, I knew my other grandparents, my father's parents, better.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the meantime, was your mother writing to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was going on--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --since you were supposed to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have-- I still have postcards that-- and not only do I have postcards that my mother wrote from, from everywhere-- to her aunt, to help them, that we should come. But my-- one cousin of mine that I never knew when I was little, she lived in Hungary. Because her father was a brother to my grandfather, and he went to Hungary. And, and in Hungary-- they, they were-- they were born in Hungary, several, I don't know, six children, or whatever. And they-- the father, her father, she survived from six. And her father wrote-- even there's a postcard she has that he wrote to my auntie in America they should help my mother come. So she has it even. In other words, from every site, everybody was trying to hurry up this process, so that my mother and her three-- we should come to, to America to join my  father.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop the tape now.
